revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Hyōgo Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24593703458|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Itatehyozu-jinja]][[en:Itatehyōzu Shrine]][[ja:射楯兵主神社]][[simple:Itatehyōzu Shrine]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Itate-Hyozu-jinja, shinmon.jpg | map_type = Japan Hyōgo Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|2.4|N|134|41|47.8|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | deity = all the deities of its province * {{ill|Chiyou|lt=Hyozu-no-Kami|ja|兵主神|en|Chiyou|ru|Чи Ю|fr|Chiyou|es|Chi You|zh|蚩尤|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiyou|qq=}} | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] }} ''''Itatehyozu Shrine''' (Japanese: 板手兵主神社, Itatehyōzu Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Himeji, Hyōgo|Himeji]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]].<ref name="Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |date=2023-06-15 |title=Hitotsuyama Shinji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9220 |access-date=2023-11-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230615234723/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9220 |archive-date=2023-06-15 }}</ref> It is located just outside of [[Himeji Castle]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Harima no Kuni Sōsha Shrine to Himeji Castle |url=https://www.google.com/maps/dir/Harima+no+Kuni+S%C5%8Dsha+Shrine+%E6%92%AD%E7%A3%A8%E5%9B%BD%E7%B7%8F%E7%A4%BE+%E5%B0%84+%E6%A5%AF%E5%85%B5%E4%B8%BB%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/Himeji+Castle,+68+Honmachi,+Himeji,+Hyōgo+670-0012,+Japan/@34.8370045,134.6950164,17z/data=!4m14!4m13!1m5!1m1!1s0x3554e0064487c157:0xa02aa10a691d1a34!2m2!1d134.6969291!2d34.8337671!1m5!1m1!1s0x3554e003a23324b3:0x7a4f8c2f6eba81b1!2m2!1d134.6939047!2d34.839449!3e3?hl=en&entry=ttu |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Harima no Kuni Sōsha Shrine to Himeji Castle}}</ref> It is renowned for its Single Mountain Rite held every 60 years and Three Mountains Rite held every 20 years. The shrine's rituals are related to rituals at [[Iwa Shrine]] a famous nearby shrine.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> It is a [[Sōja shrine]] and enshrines all the gods of [[Harima Province]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Harima-no-Kuni Sosha Shrine {{!}} Reception Halls {{!}} Himeji Convention Guide |url=https://www.himeji-kanko.jp/convention/en/reception/04/ |access-date=2023-06-24 |website=www.himeji-kanko.jp}}</ref> It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Harima Province]]. Whenever a new governor of Harima Province was appointed he would be sent to the shrine to worship all the gods of the province.<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
[[Ichinomiya]] and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined.<ref>[https://archive.today/20230428181137/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 Encyclopedia of Shinto]</ref> In this case the [[Iwa Shrine]] is the Province's Ichinomiya,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=ja}}</ref> and uniquely their rituals are highly intertwined.<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
== Rituals == The Itatehyozu Shrine follows a distinct cycle for its two principal rites. The Single Mountain Rite is performed once every sixty years, while the Three Mountains Rite takes place every twenty years.<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
This is the reverse of the rites at [[Iwa Shrine]] in which the three mountains rite is every sixty years and the single mountain rite is every twenty years.<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
It is said this similarity happened because the Iwa deity was invited to Itatehyōzu Shrine in ancient times through [[Kanjō]].<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
=== Three Mountains Rite === Every twenty years, the Three Mountains Rite takes place at Itatehyōzu Shrine. This is to worship the three mountains. [[Iwa Shrine]] in [[Shisō, Hyōgo]] also holds these rites, but it does so every sixty years instead.<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
At Itatehyōzu Shrine, there is a week-long event. A bamboo and cloth mountain is built. It is fifteen meters high. It is placed at the shrine's entrance. A small shrine is put on top of this. Food offerings are made. These offerings include mochi.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> === Single Mountain Rite === The Single Mountain Rite is a festival at Itatehyōzu Shrine. It happens every sixty years. The shrine is in Ichinomiya Town, Shisō County, Hyōgo Prefecture. The festival honors Mt. Miya. This mountain is said to be the tomb of [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
One month before the festival, a new shrine is placed on the mountain top. A white flag is raised. The festival includes worshipping three mountains from afar. These are Mt. Takahata, Mt. Hanasaki, and Mt. Shirakura. A sacred palanquin is also carried to the river valley.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> {| class="wikitable" !Shrine !Single Mountain !Three Mountains |- |Itatehyōzu Shrine |20 |60 |- |[[Iwa Shrine]] |60 |20 |}
